title_en: Salah in Tabyin al-Haqaiq: Hanafi Prayer Law with Evidence
title_id: Shalat dalam Tabyin al-Haqaiq: Hukum Shalat Hanafi beserta Dalilnya
content_id:
Bab shalat az-Zayla'i dalam Tabyin al-Haqaiq menyajikan hukum shalat mazhab Hanafi dengan analisis berlatar hadis yang membedakan karyanya. Bab ini mencakup klasifikasi Hanafi tentang elemen-elemen shalat menjadi fardhu (rukun wajib), wajib (kewajiban di bawah fardhu), sunnah, dan mustahabb -- klasifikasi yang khas Hanafi dan memiliki konsekuensi praktis penting.

Elemen-elemen fardhu (rukun wajib) shalat Hanafi adalah: takbiratul ihram (Allahu Akbar), berdiri (qiyam) bagi yang mampu, membaca bagian mana pun dari Al-Quran (tidak harus al-Fatihah, dalam pandangan Hanafi -- meskipun al-Fatihah adalah yang paling dianjurkan), ruku', dan sujud. Az-Zayla'i menjelaskan pendapat Hanafi tentang bacaan Al-Quran: bagian Al-Quran mana pun, sekecil apapun, memenuhi persyaratan minimum untuk elemen fardhu. Namun, membaca al-Fatihah adalah wajib (kewajiban yang diperlukan), dan menambahkan surah setelahnya pada dua rakaat pertama juga wajib.

Distingsi Hanafi antara fardhu dan wajib adalah inti hukum shalat mazhab dan dijelaskan secara hati-hati dalam Tabyin al-Haqaiq. Meninggalkan elemen fardhu (bahkan karena lupa) membuat shalat tidak sah dan memerlukan pengulangan penuh. Meninggalkan elemen wajib secara sengaja membuat shalat tidak sah. Meninggalkan elemen wajib karena lupa tidak membatalkan shalat tetapi memerlukan sujud sahwi sebagai perbaikan -- dan shalat tersebut kemudian dianggap sah. Sistem konsekuensi tiga tingkat ini adalah penerapan praktis dari distingsi fardhu-wajib.

Az-Zayla'i memeriksa pendapat Hanafi tentang al-Fatihah dengan perhatian khasnya pada dalil hadis. Hadis 'Tidak ada shalat bagi orang yang tidak membaca Pembuka Kitab' (Bukhari, Muslim) diakui sebagai sahih -- tetapi ditafsirkan oleh mazhab Hanafi sebagai mengindikasikan bahwa al-Fatihah adalah bacaan optimal dan perlu (wajib), bukan rukun mutlak (fardhu). Az-Zayla'i menjelaskan penalaran Hanafi: jika hadis itu menjadikan al-Fatihah syarat mutlak, maka shalat apapun yang tidak membaca al-Fatihah sepenuhnya tidak sah -- tetapi mazhab Hanafi berpendapat bahwa shalat dengan bacaan Al-Quran apapun setidaknya sah, meskipun kurang sempurna. Bobot hadis itu didaftarkan sebagai menetapkan al-Fatihah sebagai wajib.

Pendapat Hanafi tentang mengucapkan Ameen dengan pelan (sirran) dalam semua shalat dijelaskan dengan merujuk pada hadis-hadis yang mengindikasikan bahwa Nabi mengucapkannya dengan pelan. Az-Zayla'i terlibat dengan hadis-hadis yang dikutip oleh mazhab Syafi'i untuk Ameen keras dan mengevaluasi sanad-sanadnya, menyimpulkan bahwa hadis-hadis yang mendukung Ameen pelan lebih kuat atau setidaknya sama kuatnya, dan bahwa praktik mapan para Sahabat di Madinah (tidak mengucapkan Ameen keras) mendukung pendapat Hanafi.

Elemen-elemen wajib shalat Hanafi diuraikan dan dijelaskan beserta landasan hadisnya: al-Fatihah di setiap rakaat; surah tambahan atau tiga ayat pada dua rakaat pertama; tuma'ninah (diam) di setiap posisi -- wajib dalam mazhab Hanafi, berbeda dengan fardhu dalam mazhab Syafi'i; tasyahhud pertama; membaca tasyahhud itu sendiri; dan salam penutup. Analisis az-Zayla'i atas setiap elemen wajib beserta landasan hadisnya membantu pelajar memahami landasan dalil dari hukum-hukum shalat terperinci mazhab Hanafi.

Bab ini ditutup dengan sujud sahwi, yang dalam mazhab Hanafi selalu dilakukan sebelum salam terakhir (berbeda dengan mazhab Syafi'i yang melakukannya setelah salam). Az-Zayla'i menyajikan dalil hadis untuk kedua pendapat tersebut, menjelaskan preferensi Hanafi untuk sujud sebelum salam berdasarkan hadis Dzu al-Yadayn (yang ditafsirkan berbeda oleh mazhab Hanafi dari mazhab Syafi'i), dan mempertahankan pendapat Hanafi sambil mengakui bobot nyata dalil hadis untuk pendapat setelah salam.
content_en:
Az-Zayla'i's prayer chapter in Tabyin al-Haqaiq presents the Hanafi school's prayer law with the hadith-grounded analysis that distinguishes his work. The chapter covers the Hanafi classification of prayer elements into fard (obligatory pillars), wajib (necessary obligations below fard), sunnah, and mustahabb — a classification that is distinctively Hanafi and has important practical consequences.

The fard (obligatory pillar) elements of Hanafi prayer are: the opening takbir (Allahu Akbar), standing (qiyam) for those capable, recitation of any portion of the Quran (not necessarily al-Fatiha, in the Hanafi view — though al-Fatiha is the most recommended), ruku' (bowing), and sujud (prostration). Az-Zayla'i explains the Hanafi position on Quran recitation: any amount of Quran, however brief, satisfies the minimum requirement for the fard element. However, reciting al-Fatiha is a wajib (necessary obligation), and adding a surah after it in the first two rak'ahs is also wajib.

The Hanafi distinction between fard and wajib is central to the school's prayer law and is explained carefully in Tabyin al-Haqaiq. Omitting a fard element (even forgetfully) renders the prayer invalid and requires complete repetition. Omitting a wajib element deliberately renders the prayer invalid. Omitting a wajib element forgetfully does not invalidate the prayer but requires the remedial prostration of forgetfulness (sujud as-sahw) — and the prayer is then considered valid. This three-tier consequence system is the practical application of the fard-wajib distinction.

Az-Zayla'i examines the Hanafi position on al-Fatiha with his characteristic attention to the hadith evidence. The hadith 'There is no prayer for one who does not recite the Opening of the Book' (al-Bukhari, Muslim) is acknowledged as authentic — but interpreted by the Hanafi school as indicating that al-Fatiha is the optimal and necessary (wajib) recitation, not an absolute pillar (fard). Az-Zayla'i explains the Hanafi reasoning: if the hadith made al-Fatiha an absolute condition, it would follow that any prayer in which al-Fatiha is not recited is completely invalid — but the Hanafi school holds that a prayer with any Quranic recitation is at minimum valid, though deficient. The weight of the hadith is registered as establishing al-Fatiha as wajib.

The Hanafi position on saying Ameen quietly (sirran) in all prayers is explained with reference to hadiths indicating that the Prophet said it quietly. Az-Zayla'i engages with the hadiths cited by the Shafi'i school for the loud Ameen and evaluates their chains of transmission, concluding that the hadiths supporting the quiet Ameen are stronger or at least equally strong, and that the established practice of the Companions of Madinah (not saying Ameen aloud) supports the Hanafi position.

The wajib elements of Hanafi prayer are enumerated and explained with their hadith basis: al-Fatiha in every rak'ah; an additional surah or three verses in the first two rak'ahs; tuma'ninah (stillness) in each position — wajib in the Hanafi school, unlike fard as in the Shafi'i school; the first tashahhud; reciting the tashahhud itself; and the concluding salam. Az-Zayla'i's analysis of each wajib element with its hadith basis helps the student understand the evidential grounding of the Hanafi school's detailed prayer rulings.

The chapter concludes with the prostration of forgetfulness (sujud as-sahw), which in the Hanafi school is always performed before the final salam (in contrast to the Shafi'i school, which performs it after the salam). Az-Zayla'i presents the hadith evidence for both positions, explains the Hanafi preference for the pre-salam prostration based on the hadith of Dhu al-Yadayn (which the Hanafi school interprets differently from the Shafi'i school), and defends the Hanafi position while acknowledging the genuine weight of the hadith evidence for the post-salam position.
content_ar: Az-Zayla'i's الصلاة الباب in Tabyin al-Haqaiq presents the الحنفي school's الصلاة law with the الحديث-grounded analysis that distinguishes his work. The الباب covers the الحنفي classification of الصلاة elements into الفرض (obligatory pillars), الواجب (necessary obligations below الفرض), السنة, and المستحب — a classification that is distinctively الحنفي and has المهم practical consequences. The الفرض (obligatory pillar) elements of الحنفي الصلاة are: the opening التكبير (الله أكبر), standing (qiyam) for those capable, التلاوة of any portion of the القرآن الكريم (not necessarily al-Fatiha, in the الحنفي view — though al-Fatiha is the most recommended), الركوع' (bowing), and السجود (prostration). Az-Zayla'i explains the الحنفي position on القرآن الكريم التلاوة: any amount of القرآن الكريم, غير أن brief, satisfies the minimum requirement for the الفرض element. غير أن, reciting al-Fatiha is a الواجب (necessary الالتزام), and adding a السورة بعد it in the أولاً two rak'ahs is أيضاً الواجب. The الحنفي distinction between الفرض and الواجب is المحوري to the school's الصلاة law and is explained carefully in Tabyin al-Haqaiq. Omitting a الفرض element (even forgetfully) renders the الصلاة invalid and requires complete repetition. Omitting a الواجب element deliberately renders the الصلاة invalid. Omitting a الواجب element forgetfully does not invalidate the الصلاة but requires the remedial prostration of forgetfulness (السجود as-sahw) — and the الصلاة is then considered valid. This three-tier consequence system is the practical application of the الفرض-الواجب distinction. Az-Zayla'i examines the الحنفي position on al-Fatiha with his characteristic attention to the الحديث evidence. The الحديث 'There is no الصلاة for one who does not recite the Opening of the الكتاب' (البخاري, مسلم) is acknowledged as authentic — but interpreted by the الحنفي school as indicating that al-Fatiha is the optimal and necessary (الواجب) التلاوة, not an absolute pillar (الفرض). Az-Zayla'i explains the الحنفي reasoning: إذا the الحديث made al-Fatiha an absolute condition, it would follow that any الصلاة in which al-Fatiha is not recited is completely invalid — but the الحنفي school holds that a الصلاة with any القرآني التلاوة is at minimum valid, though deficient. The weight of the الحديث is registered as establishing al-Fatiha as الواجب. The الحنفي position on saying آمين quietly (sirran) in all الصلوات is explained with reference to hadiths indicating that النبي ﷺ said it quietly. Az-Zayla'i engages with the hadiths cited by the الشافعي school for the loud آمين and evaluates their chains of transmission, concluding that the hadiths supporting the quiet آمين are stronger or at least equally strong, and that the established practice of the الصحابة of المدينة المنورة (not saying آمين aloud) supports the الحنفي position.
